coredata.dc:title: Multi-modal tracking dopamine using a hybrid inorganic-organic silver nanoparticle and its cellular imaging performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Zhan
Wang, Zhuosen
Tang, Yiping
Gao, Jinwei
Zhang, Cheng Cheng
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: A novel strategy to highly sensitize terbium luminescence through 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid as the antenna ligand has been realized and the hybrid inorganic-organic probe has been assembled based on the silver nanoparticles. Its green emission is turned off in the presence of dopamine and the detection process is within 2–3 s. Cyclic voltammetry curves showed that a glassy carbon electrode modified with this lanthanide nanostructure demonstrated favorable electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of dopamine in phosphate buffer. The developed nanosensor gave rise to a linear correlation depending on dopamine concentration and the detection limit was determined to be 0.41 μM. This dual-modal (optical and electrochemical) probe exhibited excellent selectivity and enough stability. The feasibility of the terbium sensitized nanosensor for cellular imaging has also been reported. Two adherent cell lines (Hela cells and THP-1 cells) exposed to the nanoprobe demonstrated striking green luminescence and showed on-off changes in the regulation of dopamine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence
Electrochemical
Cell imaging
Lanthanide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of terbium complex functionalized silver nanoparticle
Preparation of AgNP-TbM-BA modified glassy carbon (GC) electrodes
Detection of DA in aqueous solution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Q. M. appreciates the supports from Special Project of Applicable Science and Technology of Guangdong Province (No. 2016B020240008 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21371063 ), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong ( 2015A030313378 ) and Guangdong Science and Technology plan ( 2016A050502053 ). J. W. thanks supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51571094 ) and Innovation team project by department of Education of Guangdong Province ( 2016KCXTD009 ). Z. Z. acknowledges the Scientific Research Fund of Henan Provincial Education Department ( 17A150016 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Henan ( 162300410200 ) for financial assistances.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag NPs, terbium complex and AgNP-TbM-BA in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mg/L).
Excitation (inset figure) and emission spectra of AgNP-TbM-BA in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mg/L).
Emission spectra of AgNP-TbM-BA upon addition of 10−6–10−4 M of DA in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mg/L).
Cyclic voltammetry curves of bare electrode, Ag NPs and AgNP-TbM-BA.

Fluorescent microscope images of Hela cells were incubated with AgNP-TbM-BA(10 mg/L) before (A) and after treatment with various concentration of DA (B: 50 μM and C: 100 μM) (Left column: bright field; Middle column: green channel; Right column: merged image).

Fluorescent microscope images of THP-1 cells were incubated with AgNP-TbM-BA(10 mg/L) before (A) and after treatment with various concentration of DA (B: 50 μM and C: 100 μM) (Left column: bright field; Middle column: green channel; Right column: merged image).

head.ce:title: Multi-modal tracking dopamine using a hybrid inorganic-organic silver nanoparticle and its cellular imaging performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Key Laboratory of Function-Oriented Porous Materials, Luoyang Normal University, Luoyang 471934, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Key Laboratory of Function-Oriented Porous Materials, Luoyang Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center For Transparent Conductive Materials, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departments of Physiology and Developmental Biology, University of Texas, Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX 75390-9133, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departments of Physiology and Developmental Biology, University of Texas, Southwestern Medical Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel strategy to highly sensitize terbium luminescence through 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid as the antenna ligand has been realized and the hybrid inorganic-organic probe has been assembled based on the silver nanoparticles. Its green emission is turned off in the presence of dopamine and the detection process is within 2–3 s. Cyclic voltammetry curves showed that a glassy carbon electrode modified with this lanthanide nanostructure demonstrated favorable electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of dopamine in phosphate buffer. The developed nanosensor gave rise to a linear correlation depending on dopamine concentration and the detection limit was determined to be 0.41 μM. This dual-modal (optical and electrochemical) probe exhibited excellent selectivity and enough stability. The feasibility of the terbium sensitized nanosensor for cellular imaging has also been reported. Two adherent cell lines (Hela cells and THP-1 cells) exposed to the nanoprobe demonstrated striking green luminescence and showed on-off changes in the regulation of dopamine.

ce:section.ce:para: Neurotransmitters such as dopamine (DA) could control human behavior and brain activity [1]. Abnormality in its transformation level will induce various diseases and disorders, including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and Huntington's disease [2–4]. Due to such physiological and pathophysiological effects, it is essential to develop a quantitative method to accurately measure dopamine for diagnosis and continuous monitoring of neurological disorders. Approaches for quantitative detection of DA have been reported. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis and electrochemical analysis have been developed [5–16]. Among these above-mentioned sensitive techniques, fluorescence is free from analyte consumption and no reference materials are necessary. Moreover, it is convenient to conduct remote monitoring and various forms of light outputs (emission intensity, lifetime and quantum yields) could be measured.

The strategy for the fabrication of new fluorescent materials has been focused on the design of lanthanide luminescent edifices. Based on the literatures, the group of elements possesses sharp emission bands, large Stokes shifts and extended lifetime ranges [17–19]. From last decade, considerable interests are shown to prepare lanthanide probes in chemical and biological sensing systems due to their delicate responses. Since the fluorescent indicator has been confirmed, the next step to the assembly of an integrated sensor will be the encapsulation of the optical center in a solid support. In this field, metallic nanoparticle such as silver has received much attention mainly due to its unique mechanical, thermal, biological and electrochemical properties. The corresponding biocompatibility and conductivity offered great potential in different sensor applications. Here in this work, the novel fluorescent indicator was mainly consisted of two moieties. One is 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid that will be responsible for the interaction with the guest species. The terbium ion plays an important role in expressing the recognition changes. The signal transduction has been closely related to the interactions of dopamine with the boronic acid and the green emission would be switched off. With the aim of improving the stability of sensing materials, the alternative way is the attachment of the complex structure to the hosts. 11-Mercaptoundecanoic acid (MA) was employed to bind with Ag nanoparticles through Ag-S interactions and its carboxyl group could take part in the coordination with terbium ions. The terbium signals have been suppressed in the presence of DA but other interfering substances could not induce any significant influence. In the next stage, the sensing material has been loaded onto the glassy carbon (GC) electrodes and its sensing properties were studied through electrochemical analysis. Therefore, dual modes of sensing effects could be achieved based on the entrapment of the metal complex structure into silver hosts. Additionally, the availability of the photoluminescence feature in a single entity has made this hybrid inorganic-organic optical probe exceptionally valuable in cell imaging and two adherent live cells (Hela and THP-1) staining results have been explored.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium borohydride(NaBH4), 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid (BA), 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MA), trisodiumcitrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O72·H2O), Tb4O7 (99.9%) and 3-Hydroxytyramine hydrochloride were purchased from J&K company. Terbium nitrate were obtained by dissolving Tb4O7 (99.9%) in concentrated nitric acid. The Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), Roswell Park Memorial Institute(RPMI-1640) and other cell culture reagent were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Milli-Q water (18.25 MΩ cm at 25 °C) was used directly in the experiment. Tris buffer and other cell culture reagent were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The CellTiter 96®AQueous One Solution Reagent (MTS kit) was obtained from Promega. Lysotracker Green and Annexin V-APC/7-AAD kit was purchased from the BD Biosciences (USA). All the glassware was cleaned by aqua regia (HCl:HNO3 in a 3:1 ratio by volume) and rinsed with deionized water prior to the experiments. All the other reagents were provided by Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory and used without further purification.

Fluorescence emission spectra were measured by a computer controlled HITACHI F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (the quartz cuvette had a path length of 1 cm). During the dopamine titration and stability experiments, the emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm and the peak emission at 545 nm was employed as the analytical signal. The excitation and emission slit widths were both set as 5.0 nm and photomultiplier voltage was fixed as 700 V. All spectra were corrected for instrument response. Lifetime measurement was carried out on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer equipped with a 900 W Xe-lamp. (Ex = 280 nm, Em = 545 nm). Mass spectrum (MS) was obtained on a Thermo Finnigan LCQ Deca XP Max. UV–vis spectra were recorded on an Agilent 8453 spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEOL JEM-2100HR. The dynamic light scattering result and particle size distribution were investigated by Malvern Nano-ZS90. Voltammetric measurements (including cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry) were performed on a CHI660a electrochemical system with a corresponding software package. The cell images were recorded on a fluorescent microscope (Olympus BX50, Japan, Excitation filter: 300–450 nm; 540 ± 25 nm emission filter for green emission). Elemental analysis was carried out in an Elementar Vario EL elemental analyzer. Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) and elemental mapping images were obtained by using a Phenom ProX. Flow cytometry was performed on a BD Accuri C6 Flow Cytometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of terbium complex functionalized silver nanoparticle
ce:section.ce:para: Ag NPs were prepared according to a previously published method [20]. In brief, a 20 mL of 1% (w/v) citrate solution and a 75 mL of water were added in a round bottom flask and the mixture was heated to 70 °C for 15 min 1.7 mL of 1% (w/v) AgNO3 solution was further added in the mixture, followed by the quick addition of 2 mL of 0.1% (w/v) freshly prepared NaBH4 solution. The reaction solution kept vigorous stirring for 1 h at 70 °C and cooled to room temperature. The final product was in brown-yellow color. Water was added until the volume of the dispersion reached 100 mL.

11-mercaptoundecanoic acid (MA, 21.8 mg, 0.1 mmol), 3-carboxybenzeneboronic acid (BA, 33.2 mg, 0.2 mmol), and Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (45.3 mg, 0.1 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol, and the whole mixture was titrated by 3–5 drops of NH3·H2O and was refluxed at 90◦C for 1 h. Then it was cooled to room temperature. The resulting precipitate was collected and washed twice with water to give the terbium complex (abbreviated as TbM-BA) as white powder. EA found: C, 41.74; H, 6.08%, Anal. Calcd for C27H46B2O12STb [Eu(3-carboxybenzeneboronic acid)2(11-mercaptoundecanoic acid)·2H2O]: C, 41.83; H, 5.98%. MS: m/z calcd. for C27H42B2O10STb (Eu(3-carboxybenzeneboronic acid)2(11-mercaptoundecanoic acid) 739.19; MS found: m/z 740.33 [M+H]+ (mass spectrum was given in the Supporting information, Fig. S1).

In order to obtain the hybrid material, 0.05 mmol terbium complex was added into 10 mL Ag NPs solution under vigorous stirring. The mixture kept stirring for 12 h at room temperature. Finally the obtained mixture was dialyzed for 24 h with a 1000 MWCO dialysis membrane to remove the unreacted reagents. The obtained materials were store at 4 °C for further use. The obtained probe was referred as AgNP-TbM-BA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AgNP-TbM-BA modified glassy carbon (GC) electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the preparation of silver nanomaterial modified GC electrode, the GC disk electrode (3 mm in diameter, 0.071 cm2) was polished with 0.3 and 0.05 µm alumina paste on a polishing cloth (Buehler) to produce a smooth, shiny surface and then subjected to ultrasonic cleaning for about 10 min in distilled water and ethyl alcohol. Later, it was pretreated by cleaning with piranha solution (v/v = 3/1, concentrated H2SO4/30%H2O2) for 15 min and thoroughly rinsed with water and dried with nitrogen [21]. Next, the modified electrode was prepared by dropping 5 μl above prepared material solution onto the GC electrode surface. The electrode properties were tested in a three-electrode system using the prepared electrodes as working electrode, Pt as the auxiliary electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode in the range of −0.6–0.6 V at a sweep rate of 0.1 V s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: All the used stock solutions were prepared with ultrapure water, respectively. During the detection of DA, the aqueous solution of AgNP-TbM-BA was mixed with volumes of Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4) solution and make the final concentration is 10−5 M. Then, various concentrations of DA solution were added and fluorescence intensity changes were recorded accordingly.

Hela cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin and streptomycin. Cells were maintained at 37 °C in 5% CO2. THP-1 cell lines were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics (penicillin and streptomycin) and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2. The two cell lines were incubated at standard culture condition (as mentioned above) for one day. For the experiments, cells were washed with Tris buffer (pH = 7.4) twice. In the next step, it was incubated in the fresh medium (DMEM medium with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics penicillin for Hela cells; RPMI 1640 medium with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics penicillin for THP-1 cells) containing Ag-TbM-BA (10 mg/L) at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 2 h and then was treated with 50 μM DA, 100 μM DA for 15 min. Cells were washed three times with Tris-HCl buffer and observed by using a fluorescent microscope (Olympus BX50, Japan).

Hela cells were cultured in DMEM media with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics penicillin and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2. THP-1 cell lines were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics (penicillin and streptomycin) and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2. The two cell lines within replicate 96-well plates were pre-cultured at standard culture condition for one day. After removal of the medium, cells were incubated with fresh medium ((DMEM medium with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics penicillin for Hela cells; RPMI 1640 medium with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics penicillin for THP-1 cells)) containing Ag-TbM-BA (0, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 mg/L) for another 24 h. Then 20 μl MTS solution was added to each well. After incubated with 4 h, the absorbance was measured at 490 nm using a Polarstar microplate reader.

Hela cells were cultured with Ag-TbM-BA (100 μM) in DMEM media with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics penicillin and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 2 h (THP-1 cells were treated with RPMI 1640 medium with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotics penicillin under the same conditions). Then, cells were washed and resuspended in Tris-HCl buffer, and measured by flow cytometry on a BD Accuri C6 Flow Cytometer. To investigate apoptosis analysis, cells were stained with Annexin V-APC/7-AAD and dispersed within replicated 12-well microtiter plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. After removal of the medium, cells were incubated with fresh medium (here fresh medium refers to DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics penicillin or RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics penicillin) containing Ag-TbM-BA (10 mg/L) for another 24 h. After washing with binding buffer twice, centrifuging, and adding another 100 μl binding buffer, 3 μl of 7-AAD and Annexin V-PE were added to each sample. Samples were incubated for 15 min and analyzed by a BD Accuri C6 Flow Cytometer.

Fig. S2 demonstrates the recognition process in the current research. The silver surface was treated with the ethanol solution of 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid. The binding potential of thiol-containing organic molecule (referring to 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid) is effective and it efficiently adsorb onto the silver surface through covalent attachment. Furthermore, 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid has a long and soft alkyl chain, which will be useful for the generation of self-assembled monolayers. Thereafter, its remaining carboxyl unit and the organic chromophore (4-boronobenzoic acid) could participate into the coordination with terbium ions. The resultant covalently-bonded organic-inorganic sensing structure with striking green emission has been prepared ( Fig. 1).

UV–vis absorption spectra of the terbium complex and the derived Ag hybrid material were measured between 200 and 700 nm. As provided in Fig. 2, the formation of silver nanoparticle has been supported by the predominant band at around 400 nm. In the short wavelength range, a new peak located at 276 nm appeared in the curve of AgNP-TbM-BA and it might be derived from the attached terbium complex. It is observed that pure metal complex also generated the absorption band at 276 nm. Hence, the collected results indicated that lanthanide emissive species have been incorporated into noble metal nanostructure. With the aim of clarifying this point, the absorption signals of the terbium complex (TbM-BA) at different concentrations were recorded. The correlation between the UV absorbance at 276 nm and the concentration was normalized by the linear regression equation, which was consistent with the linear relationship (Fig. S3). The standard linear curve was used for the quantitative analysis. In this way, the loading efficiency of terbium complex structure was calculated as ~ 11.5%.

TEM has been used for the exploration of the formation of silver nanoclusters and the growth of particles ( Fig. 3). The prepared hybrid nanoprobe (AgNP-TbM-BA) is very stable in solution at ambient conditions and no aggregation effects are seen after seven days. Ag nanoparticles are found with the size between 10 and 20 nm. Moreover, chemical modification of terbium complex onto silver framework has very minor influence on their microstructures. We have to mention that 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid acts as a crucial role in the capping agent in aqueous solution. In the absence of such protection reagent, the particle will seriously aggregate together and the microstructures could not be controlled (Fig. S4). The size distribution of the particles was further explored by dynamic light scattering and the results supported the average size was around 15 nm (Fig. S5). The chemical composition of the hybrid material (AgNP-TbM-BA) was studied by Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) analysis (Fig. S6 and Table S1). The results showed that all the elements including C, N, O, B, Ag, S and Tb were present in the hybrid network and no other impurities were detected. It indicated that the terbium complex structure has been attached to the silver nanoparticles.

The photoluminescence and sensing performance of AgNP-TbM-BA were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy (10 mg/L in Tris-HCl buffer solution). The excitation profile has been assigned to the main contribution of 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid. A very broad and intense band between 240 and 310 nm was recorded by fixing the emission wavelength at 545 nm ( Fig. 4 inset). During this wide excitation range, different excitation wavelengths were employed to sensitize the terbium emissions and the optimized result would be pumped at 280 nm. Lanthanide emission bands are quite sharp in contrast to organic chromophores ( Fig. 5). Narrow-width emission peaks (λex = 280 nm) with maximum at 492, 547, 587, and 625 nm can be observed clearly. These bands are attributed to the 5D4 → 7F6,5D4 → 7F5,5D4 → 7F4, and 5D4 → 7F3 transitions of Tb3+ions, respectively. Stability experiments were also studied. When temperature has been increased from 25 °C to 70 °C, the fluorescence intensity varied slightly (Fig. S7). The results indicated that the probe will be useful in this temperature range. Fig. S8 gives rise to the dependence of integrated intensity ratio versus the time duration (100 h). The results revealed that the leaching of the emissive species in the hybrid material could be avoided and the reduction in luminescence ratio was less than 6%.

Emissive lanthanide structures have been explored for their potentials in the construction of responsive systems or luminescent tags [22]. Upon the titration of DA at concentration from 10−6 to 10−4 M, the emission intensities from AgNP-TbM-BA were rapidly quenched (Fig. 5). This unique change could also be observed by naked-eye under the irradiation at 254 nm (Fig. 1). In order to investigate the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of DA, we have found that a linear relation with the equation Y = 0.914 + 0.721X has been achieved (Fig. S9). The detection limit (3.3 × 10−6 M) was calculated according to the equation DL = 3 × SD/slope. SD corresponds to the standard deviation of the blank sample (obtained by 10 consecutive scans of the blank sample). Selectivity is considered as another important factor to exploit their specific properties. To make sure whether AgNP-TbM-BA was responsive to DA solely, the influence of other kinds of common coexisting substances (such as amino acids, cations and anions) were investigated. As given in Fig. S10, no obvious changes could be observed after the addition of other coexisting substances, suggesting the sensing process would be switched off by the presence of DA.

This quenching deactivation of terbium fluorescence has been discussed and the mutual interactions would contribute to the sensing processes (Fig. 1). It is accepted that the inner-shell 4f-4f transitions of lanthanide ions are forbidden and the observed energy migration path starts from the harvested energy of coordinated ligand [23]. If the external analytes could interfere with the functional groups of the organic structure and the absorbed energy would be affected. In this way, the metal-centered luminescence derived from antenna effects would be substantially suppressed. In this work, 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid has been selected as the chromophore. At the meantime, boronic acid and its derivatives will be easy to react with 1,2-, 1,3- or 1,4-diol to form a stable cyclic ester structure [24–27]. This stimuli-responsive feature would impede the intra-molecular energy transfer and lead to the emission quench. Here we focused on the fluorescence performance of the BA and investigated the titration experiments between ligand BA and DA (Figs. S11 and S12). We can see that BA has a strong emission peak with the maximum at 339 nm excited at as 304 nm. The presence of DA also has a negative influence on its own emission (Fig. S12). Simultaneously, the other guest molecules did not induce the same effect. Accordingly, the intra-molecular energy transfer was prohibited since the absorbed ultra-violet energy donated by the organic fluorophore was dissipated. Hence, the terbium green emission was quenched.

The lifetime is an important parameter and it determines the close interactions between the fluorophore and the analyte. In the absence of dopamine, the excited lifetime of AgNP-TbM-BA was measured to be 0.85 ms (Fig. S13). However, the excited state was deactivated upon contact with dopamine and the lifetime has been reduced to 0.12 ms. According to the theoretical description [28], the prominent decrease in lifetime values supported the “on-off” change was attributed to dynamic quenching process.

In recent years, electrochemical analysis approach as an effective method has been employed in host-guest chemistry studies. It is known that silver is a classic noble metal, which possesses excellent conductivity. Consequently, silver nanomaterial as electrode materials has received considerable interests in the field of electrochemistry. Silver nanoprobes are regarded as striking candidates for functional electrodes. The silver containing hybrid material was successfully assembled onto GC electrodes and its electrochemical sensing properties were measured in a three-electrode system using Pt as the auxiliary electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The cyclic voltammetry (potential range −0.6 to 0.6 V; sweep rate of 0.1 V s−1) was used to explore the electrochemical changes of the modified GC electrodes. The responses of AgNP-TbM-BA modified glass carbon electrode were compared with the bare GC electrode and AgNP modified GC electrode for a more extensive study. The results clearly demonstrated that the peak current of bare electrode was much lower than the terbium complex containing hybrid materials ( Fig. 6). It firmly supported that the covalently bonded organic structures and terbium ions would improve the electrocatalytic activity and facilitate the transport of electrons between molecules and electrode surface.

To evaluate the recognition application of this hybrid platform, the detection of dopamine on the AgNP–TbM–BA/GCE was measured by cyclic voltammetry in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 7.4). As provided in Fig. S14, the redox signals of AgNP-TbM-BA/GCE were markedly increasing in the presence of DA. The oxidation and reduction currents were specific to the addition of DA from 1 to 10 μM. These results are consistent with the photoluminescence detections and the corresponding relation curve has been given in Fig. S15. Based on the collected results, the relationship between the electrochemical signal and the DA concentration belongs to the linear equation. The resultant detection limit is determined to be 0.41 μM in terms of equation of S/N = 3. Compare to the fluorescence measurements, its detection limit is much lower.

The development of nanometer scale particles for cellular uptake has given rise to an important issue in biomedical studies. The approach for clarifying interactions of nanoparticles with cells in vitro provides an efficient and accessible way to investigate cellular responses. In contrast to conventional organic chromophores, lanthanide optical nanoprobes exhibit narrow emissions and long excited lifetime. In this study, we have demonstrated that AgNP-TbM-BA can be applied to recognize intracellular dopamine (
Figs. 7 and 8). After the two cell lines (Hela cells and THP-1 cells) were incubated with AgNP-TbM-BA for 2 h, fluorescence microscopic images showed very strong intracellular green luminescence. These results suggested that AgNP-TbM-BA could play key roles in target imaging. Moreover, the green emissions were quickly responsive to the exposure of 50 or 100 μM of DA, indicating that dopamine can be detected inside living cells. This promising capability of nanostructured scaffolding system for the purpose of cellular imaging pathway has proved for the first time that lanthanide hybrid nanosensor based on silver host could be considered for the uses in specific biomedical applications.

In order to study the influence of Ag-TbM-BA on the viability of cells, the flow cytometric analysis was employed (Fig. S16). According to the previous literatures [29], very limited early apoptotic cells and late apoptotic or dead cells were found in Q1 and Q2 region. The results suggested the hybrid material gave rise to negligible cytotoxicity to the cells.

To further clarify the effects of toxicity on live cells, viability (MTS) assay was used (Fig. S17). Following incubation with Ag-TbM-BA (100 μM) for 24 h, the cellular viabilities of HeLa cells and THP-1 cells were well above 90%, indicating that the incorporated luminescent species had very low toxicity.

In summary, organic terbium(III) complex with 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid has been synthesized and it has been incorporated into silver nanostructure that the hybrid material shows bright green emission and narrow bandwidth. This makes it excellent probe for sensing dopamine via measurement of luminescence changes. At the same time, electrochemical performances of the electrode modified by the hybrid probe have been investigated by cyclic voltammetry. The linear response for dopamine has been realized in the concentration range of 1–10 μM. Moreover, this hybrid inorganic-organic material shows promise as a functional nanoprobe for cellular imaging and it gives rise to an on-off change in the presence of dopamine within two live cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q. M. appreciates the supports from Special Project of Applicable Science and Technology of Guangdong Province (No. 2016B020240008), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21371063), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong (2015A030313378) and Guangdong Science and Technology plan (2016A050502053). J. W. thanks supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (51571094) and Innovation team project by department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009). Z. Z. acknowledges the Scientific Research Fund of Henan Provincial Education Department (17A150016) and Natural Science Foundation of Henan (162300410200) for financial assistances.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.08.045.
